items.q: Why do atoms emit light only at specific wavelengths rather than a continuous spectrum?
items.correct: Electrons occupy discrete energy levels; transitions between levels emit photons of specific frequencies (E = hf).
items.misconception: Atoms can emit any wavelength, but only certain ones are visible.
items.random_wrong: Atoms emit only one colour each, determined by their temperature.
items.why: Students don't internalise the quantisation of energy levels; without it, line spectra look arbitrary instead of a direct consequence of quantum mechanics.

items.q: What determines the energy of a single photon?
items.correct: Its frequency (E = hf) — only frequency, not intensity.
items.misconception: A brighter light has higher-energy photons because intensity makes each photon more energetic.
items.random_wrong: Photon energy depends on how fast the photon is moving relative to the source.
items.why: Students conflate intensity (number of photons) with photon energy (frequency-dependent); this is why dim UV is more dangerous than bright red.

items.q: How does the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons depend on light intensity?
items.correct: It does not — max KE depends only on the frequency of the light; intensity affects the rate (number) of electrons emitted.
items.misconception: Higher intensity gives photoelectrons more kinetic energy.
items.random_wrong: Intensity has no effect on anything in the photoelectric effect.
items.why: Students confuse 'brighter light' with 'more energetic photons'; intensity gives more photons, but each one's energy is set by frequency.

items.q: Electron drops from −3.4 eV to −13.6 eV. Photon energy (eV)?
items.why: The real figure is 10.2. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
